title: ML ensemble with PF 1.45 out-of-sample still lost money live. Why the AI wasn't the bottleneck.
author: xXDADDYTHRASHERXx
selftext:
Solo dev, laptop-only, six months into a forex bot project. Wanted to share a lesson because I see a lot of "will ML save my trading bot" questions here and my honest answer is "only if your non-ML infrastructure is already correct."



\## The AI stack that didn't save us



V3 bot (\~7,200 lines Python) uses:

\- XGBoost + CatBoost + LightGBM ensemble as the entry filter

\- SHAP for feature importance (#1 feature: H1 EMA distance; 21 one-hot encoded categorical features turned out near-zero)

\- Optuna hyperparameter sweeps

\- Walk-forward validation on out-of-sample windows



Backtest showed PF 1.45 with those ML entries gating a classical TA-confluence signal. I was proud of it.



\## 17 days live, 45 trades later



\- Profit factor: 0.54

\- Win rate: 62% (winning more than half and still losing money)

\- P&L: -40.6 pips



This is the scenario the ML-skeptics on Reddit warn about, and I had lived through it. Except — the ML wasn't the problem.



\## The audit



I pulled every one of those 45 trades (exact entry price, exact signal) and replayed them through the same simulator with a \*different exit rule\*. Same ML. Same entries. Just the exit changed.



\- Phase A: wide stop at 2× ATR

\- Phase B: at MFE +10p, move stop to entry +3

\- Phase C: fixed TP at 2.75R, no trailing



Same 45 entries, different exit: \*\*PF 1.60, +152.5 pips, 60% WR.\*\*



The ML ensemble had picked the right trades. The exit logic was giving all the edge back.



\## What I'd tell someone adding ML to their bot



\- \*\*Your non-ML infrastructure has to be correct first.\*\* An XGBoost entry filter on top of a broken exit is worse than no ML at all, because the better entries just give you more losing trades at the same loss per trade.

\- \*\*SHAP is more useful than the model.\*\* The biggest ML win for me wasn't the classifier — it was seeing that 21 categorical feature dummies were near-zero in SHAP, which meant my feature engineering was wasting compute.

\- \*\*OOS walk-forward is not enough.\*\* My walk-forward PF was 1.45. Live was 0.54. The gap wasn't overfitting — it was 15 separate bugs where the simulator and the live engine had diverged (stale config merges, unit caps clamping position sizes, dynamic risk multipliers stacking, old exits racing new ones). Walk-forward doesn't catch code-path divergence.

\- \*\*Flat risk beat every "AI-informed risk scaling" scheme I tried.\*\* Kelly-VAPS + ML confidence + regime probability + MTF conviction compounded multiplicatively and silently halved my real risk during losing streaks. Constant 1% risk after the audit outperformed.



\## Why I'm posting



I was 48 hours from launching a paid signal service when the audit hit. PF 0.54 is not something you charge people for. So I pulled the pricing tiers, took the product page down, and instead committed to publishing every trade the bot takes publicly for accountability.



Lifetime PF is still 0.46 — the early losing strategies are still in the ledger. I'm not selling anything, not selling signals, and not selling a course. The point is accountability: if the post-audit config holds for 30 days, the numbers will earn themselves. If it doesn't, you'll see that too.



Happy to dig into: the ensemble training pipeline, SHAP readout, Optuna search spaces, the exit audit simulator, the 15 sim-vs-live bugs, or the walk-forward pipeline that didn't catch the divergence.



Live ledger (no signup, auto-updated from bot logs every 30 min): [https://thrasherapps.com/signals.html](https://thrasherapps.com/signals.html)
